seo.title: HTML Lists and Tables - Complete Guide with Examples | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn HTML lists (ul, ol, dl) and tables (table, thead, tbody, tfoot) with colspan, rowspan, and caption. Includes 55 practice questions for beginners.
seo.keywords: html lists, ordered list, unordered list, html tables, colspan, rowspan, thead tbody tfoot, html list tutorial, html table tutorial, html dl dt dd

content.what:
What Are HTML Lists and Tables?

Websites are full of lists and tables. The menu on a restaurant page is a list. The nutrition facts on a food label are a table. The steps in a recipe are a list. The schedule of classes at a school is a table. HTML gives us special tags to mark these up the right way.

Three Types of Lists

HTML has three kinds of lists, each for a different situation:

  • Unordered lists (<ul>) — when the order does not matter. Shopping list, features of a product, tags on a blog post. Displayed with bullets.
  • Ordered lists (<ol>) — when the order matters. Steps in a recipe, top 10 movies, instructions to assemble a chair. Displayed with numbers.
  • Description lists (<dl>) — for pairs of terms and their definitions. A glossary, a FAQ, a dictionary.

What Are Tables?

A table is a grid of rows and columns. It is the right choice only when you have real data that belongs in rows and columns — student marks, cricket scores, train timings, a price comparison. Tables are NOT for laying out pages. That was done in the 1990s and it was a terrible idea. Today we use CSS (Flexbox and Grid) for layout.
content.why:
Why Should You Learn Lists and Tables?

1. They Give Content Meaning

When you mark a shopping list as a <ul>, screen readers for blind users announce "list with 5 items" before reading them. A search engine knows these are related items. Without the list tags, it is just text. Meaning matters.

2. You Get Free Styling

Browsers automatically add bullets to <ul>, numbers to <ol>, and borders to tables. You do not have to style them from scratch — they look decent even without CSS.

3. Tables Are Still Everywhere

Every dashboard, every report, every price comparison, every leaderboard uses tables. If you want to build dashboards or show data in the future, you need to know tables cold.

4. Accessibility and SEO

Properly marked up lists and tables make your page readable by screen readers and easier for Google to understand. A table with <th> headers is much more useful to Google than a messy grid of <div>s.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

Unordered Lists

An unordered list uses the <ul> tag. Inside it, each item is wrapped in an <li> (list item) tag. Browsers show each item with a bullet point.

<ul>
  <li>Milk</li>
  <li>Bread</li>
  <li>Eggs</li>
</ul>

You can have as many <li> items as you want. The order of items does not imply any ranking — the items are just related.

Ordered Lists

An ordered list uses <ol>. Each item is still an <li>. Browsers automatically number the items 1, 2, 3, and so on.

<ol>
  <li>Wash your hands</li>
  <li>Chop the onions</li>
  <li>Fry them in oil</li>
</ol>

Useful Attributes on <ol>

  • start — starts counting from a different number. <ol start="5"> starts at 5.
  • type — changes the numbering style. type="1" (default), type="A" (A, B, C), type="a" (a, b, c), type="I" (I, II, III), type="i" (i, ii, iii).
  • reversed — counts backwards. Useful for a countdown like "Top 10" that starts at 10.

Description Lists

Description lists are for term-definition pairs. You use three tags: <dl> for the whole list, <dt> for the term (definition term), and <dd> for the definition.

<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dd>The language used to structure web pages.</dd>
  <dt>CSS</dt>
  <dd>The language used to style web pages.</dd>
</dl>

Nested Lists

You can put a list inside a list item. This creates a nested list — a list inside a list. Perfect for outlines, hierarchies, and nested menus. Always put the inner list inside an <li>, not between list items.

<ul>
  <li>Fruits
    <ul>
      <li>Apple</li>
      <li>Banana</li>
    </ul>
  </li>
  <li>Vegetables</li>
</ul>

Tables

Tables use several tags working together:

  • <table> — wraps the entire table.
  • <tr> — table row. One per row.
  • <th> — table header cell. Used in the header row. Browsers make these bold and centered.
  • <td> — table data cell. Normal cells.
  • <thead> — wraps the header row(s).
  • <tbody> — wraps the body rows.
  • <tfoot> — wraps the footer row(s), like totals.
  • <caption> — the title of the table. Place it right after <table>.

colspan and rowspan

Sometimes a cell needs to span multiple columns or rows. Use colspan to make a cell stretch across columns, and rowspan to make it stretch down rows.

<td colspan="2">This cell is 2 columns wide</td>
<td rowspan="3">This cell is 3 rows tall</td>

Tables Are for Data, Not Layouts

In the old days (1995-2005), people used tables to position things on a page — a header in one row, a sidebar and content in two cells of the next row, a footer in the third row. This was a disaster for accessibility, mobile phones, and maintenance. Never use tables for layout. Use CSS Flexbox or Grid instead. Tables are only for tabular data — rows and columns of related values.

Styling Tables (Preview)

By default, tables look plain — cells with no borders, no padding. A few CSS lines transform them. You will learn CSS properly in Chapter 9, but here is a preview of what makes tables look good: border on each cell, border-collapse: collapse on the table, padding inside cells, and alternating row colors for readability.

codeExamples.title: A Simple Shopping List
codeExamples.explanation: This is a classic unordered list. The <ul> tag creates the list, and each <li> is one item. The browser automatically adds bullet points before each item. The items are not in any particular order — it does not matter whether you buy milk or bread first. Save this as shopping.html and open it in your browser.
codeExamples.output:
A page with the heading 'Aarav's Shopping List' and a bulleted list:
- Milk
- Bread
- Eggs
- Bananas
- Chocolate

codeExamples.title: Recipe Steps with an Ordered List
codeExamples.code:
How to Make Masala Chai

Priya's five-step recipe:

  1. Boil one cup of water in a pan.
  2. Add half a teaspoon of crushed ginger and 2 cardamom pods.
  3. Add 1 teaspoon of tea leaves.
  4. Pour in half a cup of milk and boil for 2 minutes.
  5. Strain into a cup and add sugar to taste.
codeExamples.explanation: This uses <ol> because the order matters — you cannot pour the tea before boiling the water. Each step is in a <li>. The browser automatically numbers them 1 through 5. If Priya adds a sixth step later, the numbering updates automatically.
codeExamples.output:
How to Make Masala Chai
Priya's five-step recipe:
1. Boil one cup of water in a pan.
2. Add half a teaspoon of crushed ginger and 2 cardamom pods.
3. Add 1 teaspoon of tea leaves.
4. Pour in half a cup of milk and boil for 2 minutes.
5. Strain into a cup and add sugar to taste.

codeExamples.title: Ordered List Attributes: start, type, reversed
codeExamples.code:
Rohan's Top 5 Movies (Countdown)

  1. 3 Idiots
  2. Dangal
  3. Lagaan
  4. Sholay
  5. Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara

Chapters (Starting from 5)

  1. Lists and Tables
  2. Forms and Inputs
  3. Semantic HTML

Plan Options (A, B, C)

  1. Basic Plan
  2. Pro Plan
  3. Premium Plan

Roman Numeral List

  1. Introduction
  2. Main Content
  3. Conclusion
codeExamples.explanation: This demonstrates all three ordered list attributes. The reversed attribute counts from 5 down to 1 — perfect for a top-5 countdown. start="5" begins numbering at 5 instead of 1. type="A" uses capital letters A, B, C. type="I" uses Roman numerals I, II, III. Other valid types are a, i, and 1 (default).
codeExamples.output: Four lists shown: a reversed 5-4-3-2-1 countdown, a list starting at 5, a letter list (A, B, C), and a Roman numeral list (I, II, III).

codeExamples.title: Description List for a Glossary
codeExamples.code:
Web Development Glossary

HTML
HyperText Markup Language. Used to create the structure of web pages.
CSS
Cascading Style Sheets. Used to style and design web pages.
JavaScript
A programming language that makes web pages interactive.
Browser
A program like Chrome, Firefox, or Edge that displays web pages.
codeExamples.explanation: A description list (<dl>) is perfect for glossaries, FAQs, metadata, and any term-definition content. <dt> holds the term being defined, and <dd> holds the description. Browsers typically show <dd> indented below its <dt>. One term can have multiple <dd> tags if it has multiple definitions.
codeExamples.output: A glossary where each term (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Browser) appears on its own line, with its definition indented below it.

codeExamples.title: Nested List for a Menu Outline
codeExamples.code:
Spice Palace Menu

  • Starters
    • Samosa
    • Paneer Tikka
    • Vegetable Pakora
  • Main Course
    • Butter Chicken
    • Dal Makhani
    • Paneer Butter Masala
  • Desserts
    • Gulab Jamun
    • Rasmalai
    • Kulfi
codeExamples.explanation: Nested lists let you create hierarchies. Notice that the inner <ul> is placed inside an outer <li>, after its text. This is the correct way. Never place a <ul> directly between two <li> tags — that is invalid HTML. You can nest as deeply as you want, but more than 3 levels usually becomes confusing.
codeExamples.output: A menu with 3 top-level items (Starters, Main Course, Desserts), each with 3 nested items shown indented below.

codeExamples.title: A Student Marks Table with thead, tbody, tfoot
codeExamples.code:
Class 8A - Term 1 Marks
Student Maths Science English
Aarav Sharma 92 88 85
Priya Patel 95 91 89
Rohan Kumar 78 82 80
Average 88.3 87.0 84.7
codeExamples.explanation: This is a complete data table showing three students and their marks in three subjects. The <caption> is the table title. <thead> holds the header row with <th> cells (bold and centered by default). <tbody> holds the data rows. <tfoot> holds the summary row (averages). A bit of CSS gives it borders, padding, and colored header and footer. Save as marks.html and open it — it works.
codeExamples.output: A table with a title at the top, a pink header row (Student, Maths, Science, English), three data rows for Aarav, Priya, and Rohan, and a green footer row with the averages.

codeExamples.title: Using colspan and rowspan
codeExamples.code:
Ananya's School Timetable

Time Monday Tuesday Wednesday
9:00 Maths Science English
10:00 School Assembly (all classes)
11:00 History Double Art Period Geography
12:00 Hindi Music
1:00 Lunch Break
codeExamples.explanation: This timetable demonstrates both colspan and rowspan. The 10:00 and 1:00 rows use colspan="3" because those events cover all three days. The Art period uses rowspan="2" because it lasts two hours. Notice that in the row following a rowspan, you do NOT write a <td> for the cell that is already occupied — it is already there from the previous row.
codeExamples.output: A timetable with a spanning 'School Assembly' cell across all three day columns, a 'Double Art Period' cell spanning two rows on Tuesday, and a 'Lunch Break' cell spanning all three days with a yellow background.

commonMistakes.title: Placing a Nested List Between li Tags
commonMistakes.error: Invalid HTML. The nested ul is directly inside ul, not inside li.
commonMistakes.explanation: A <ul> can only contain <li> elements as its direct children. To nest a list, you must put the inner <ul> inside one of the <li> tags, after its text. Browsers may still render the bad version, but it is invalid HTML and causes problems with accessibility and validators.

commonMistakes.title: Using Tables for Page Layout
commonMistakes.error: No error, but this is wrong for many reasons: bad for accessibility, bad for mobile, hard to maintain, bad for SEO.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
Logo
Menu
Sidebar
Main content goes here...
commonMistakes.explanation: Tables are for data, not for arranging things on a page. Using tables for layout was common in the 1990s but is now considered harmful. Screen readers read tables cell-by-cell, which makes no sense for a layout. It is nearly impossible to make table-based layouts responsive for mobile phones. Use semantic tags and CSS (Flexbox, Grid) for layout.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting the tr Tag Around Cells
commonMistakes.error: Browsers try to auto-fix this, but the structure is broken. Cells appear in unexpected places.
commonMistakes.explanation: Every row of cells in a table must be wrapped in a <tr> (table row) tag. Cells (<th> or <td>) cannot be direct children of <table>. Forgetting <tr> is a very common beginner mistake that produces a broken table.

commonMistakes.title: Writing a td in a Row Already Covered by rowspan
commonMistakes.wrongCode: Merged A Merged B
commonMistakes.error: The table shifts and the cells appear in the wrong columns.
commonMistakes.correctCode: Merged A B
commonMistakes.explanation: When a cell uses rowspan, the cells below it in that column are already "filled." So in the next row, you should NOT include a <td> for that column. If a table has 3 columns and one cell uses rowspan="2", the next row will only have 2 <td> tags, not 3.

commonMistakes.title: Using ul Instead of ol for Instructions
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
How to Install the App

  • Download the file
  • Open the installer
  • Click Next
  • Click Finish
commonMistakes.error: No error, but wrong semantically. The order matters for instructions.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
How to Install the App

  1. Download the file
  2. Open the installer
  3. Click Next
  4. Click Finish
commonMistakes.explanation: Use <ol> whenever the order is important — instructions, steps, recipes, rankings. Use <ul> only when the items are truly interchangeable. For installation steps, step 3 must come after step 2, so it is an ordered list.

summary: HTML has three types of lists: <ul> for unordered, <ol> for ordered, and <dl> for description lists.
Every list item inside <ul> and <ol> must be wrapped in an <li> tag. Description lists use <dt> for terms and <dd> for definitions.
<ol> supports useful attributes: start to begin at a different number, type to change the style (1, A, a, I, i), and reversed to count backwards.
You can nest lists by placing a <ul> or <ol> inside an <li> tag, not between two <li> tags.
Tables use <table> as the wrapper, <tr> for each row, <th> for header cells, and <td> for data cells.
Use <thead> for the header row(s), <tbody> for the data rows, and <tfoot> for summary or totals. Add a <caption> right after <table> for the title.
colspan makes a cell span multiple columns, rowspan makes it span multiple rows. Remember to skip the covered cells in following rows.
Tables are for tabular data only. Never use tables for page layout — use CSS Flexbox or Grid instead. Tables for layout are bad for accessibility, mobile, and SEO.
By default, browsers add bullets to <ul>, numbers to <ol>, and no borders to tables. A small amount of CSS makes them look great.

topicSpecific.hint: ul is an unordered list. What do unordered lists use for markers?
topicSpecific.answer: A bulleted list with three items: Red, Green, Blue.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <ul> tag creates an unordered list, and each <li> is one item. Browsers display bullet points before each item.

topicSpecific.hint: ol stands for ordered list.
topicSpecific.answer: A numbered list: 1. First, 2. Second, 3. Third.
topicSpecific.explanation: <ol> creates an ordered list. Browsers automatically add numbers 1, 2, 3 before each <li>.

topicSpecific.question: Create an unordered list of your three favourite fruits inside a complete HTML page.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <ul> and three <li> tags inside the body.
topicSpecific.explanation: Wrap the three items in <ul> and </ul>. Each item goes in its own <li>.

topicSpecific.hint: The start attribute changes the first number.
topicSpecific.explanation: The start="3" attribute tells the browser to start counting at 3 instead of 1. So Apple is number 3 and Banana is number 4.

topicSpecific.hint: The type attribute changes the numbering style.
topicSpecific.answer:
A. Option one
B. Option two
C. Option three
topicSpecific.explanation: type="A" uses capital letters instead of numbers. Use a for lowercase, I for Roman numerals, i for lowercase Roman.

topicSpecific.question: Create a shopping list with nested items. Top level: Groceries and Electronics. Under Groceries: Milk and Bread. Under Electronics: Headphones and Charger.
topicSpecific.hint: Put a nested <ul> inside the parent <li>.
topicSpecific.explanation: To nest, put a new <ul> inside the parent <li>, after its text. The nested list displays indented with its own bullets.

topicSpecific.hint: Where should the nested ul go?
topicSpecific.explanation: The nested <ul> must be inside the <li>, not between li tags. A ul can only directly contain li elements.

topicSpecific.question: Create a description list with two terms: 'HTML' (meaning: structure of web pages) and 'CSS' (meaning: style of web pages).
topicSpecific.hint: Use <dl>, <dt>, and <dd>.
topicSpecific.answer:
<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dd>Structure of web pages</dd>
  <dt>CSS</dt>
  <dd>Style of web pages</dd>
</dl>
topicSpecific.explanation: Description lists are great for glossaries. <dt> is the term, <dd> is the description. They go in pairs inside <dl>.

topicSpecific.hint: Count the tr tags.
topicSpecific.explanation: Each <tr> creates one row. This table has 3 tr tags, so 3 rows: one header row (A, B) and two data rows (1, 2 and 3, 4).

topicSpecific.question: Build a table showing 3 students with their marks. Columns: Name and Marks. Students: Aarav (92), Priya (88), Rohan (75).
topicSpecific.hint: Use <table>, <tr>, <th> for headers, and <td> for data.
topicSpecific.explanation: The table has 4 rows: one header row with <th> cells, and three data rows with <td> cells. The border="1" attribute adds a simple border so you can see the structure (CSS is preferred, but this works).

topicSpecific.hint: The reversed attribute counts backwards.
topicSpecific.explanation: The reversed attribute makes the numbering go from 3 down to 1 instead of 1 to 3. This is useful for countdowns.

topicSpecific.question: Write an ordered list of the steps to make a cup of tea. At least 4 steps.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <ol> because the order matters.
topicSpecific.answer:
<ol>
  <li>Boil water in a pan.</li>
  <li>Add tea leaves.</li>
  <li>Add milk and sugar.</li>
  <li>Strain into a cup and serve.</li>
</ol>
topicSpecific.explanation: Steps in a recipe must be in order, so an ordered list is correct. Unordered list would be wrong here.

topicSpecific.question: Which tag makes text bold and centered in a table by default?
topicSpecific.hint: It is a header cell.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <th> (table header) tag makes its content bold and centered by default. Use it for column or row headers. Use <td> for normal data cells.

topicSpecific.hint: Table cells must be wrapped in something.
topicSpecific.explanation: Every group of cells must be wrapped in a <tr> (table row). Without tr, the cells have no row structure and the table is broken.

topicSpecific.question: Create a nested list: top level items are 'Cricket' and 'Football'. Under Cricket list two players: Virat and Rohit. Under Football list two players: Messi and Ronaldo.
topicSpecific.hint: Nest a <ul> inside each <li>.
topicSpecific.explanation: The nested list goes inside the li, after the parent item's text. Each sport has its own sub-list of players.

topicSpecific.hint: colspan=2 means the cell stretches across 2 columns.
topicSpecific.answer: A table with 2 rows. The first row has one cell 'Title' spanning both columns. The second row has two cells: A and B.
topicSpecific.explanation: The colspan="2" attribute merges the cell across 2 columns, making it look like a single wide cell above two narrow cells.

topicSpecific.question: Create a table with caption 'Cricket Scores', columns Match and Runs, and 3 rows of data. Include thead and tbody.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <caption>, <thead>, <tbody>.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <caption> is the table title — it goes right after <table>. <thead> holds the header row and <tbody> holds the data rows.

topicSpecific.hint: The first column is already filled by the rowspan.
topicSpecific.answer: 2 td cells (the rowspan already fills the first column).
topicSpecific.explanation: The first cell of row 2 is already occupied by the rowspan from row 1. So the second row only needs 2 td cells for the remaining 2 columns. Writing 3 td cells would shift everything and break the table.

topicSpecific.question: Build a 3-column, 3-row timetable. Columns: Day, Period 1, Period 2. Days: Monday (Maths, Science), Tuesday (English, History), Wednesday (Hindi, Art).
topicSpecific.hint: Header row + 3 data rows = 4 tr elements.
topicSpecific.explanation: 4 rows total: one header with th cells and three data rows with td cells. Each row has 3 cells for the 3 columns.

topicSpecific.question: Which list type should you use for a list of ingredients in a recipe, where the order does not matter?
topicSpecific.hint: Order does not matter.
topicSpecific.explanation: Ingredients are a set of items with no specific order, so <ul> is correct. The steps of a recipe would use <ol> because order matters there.

topicSpecific.hint: When a cell uses rowspan, don't repeat it in the next row.
topicSpecific.explanation: The first column of row 2 is already filled by the rowspan. Writing another 'Header' td pushes cell B into the wrong column. Remove the duplicate.

topicSpecific.question: Create a table with a row that spans 3 columns across the top. Title: 'Student Report'. Below it, 3 columns: Subject, Marks, Grade. Add 2 students.
topicSpecific.hint: Use colspan=3 on the title cell.
topicSpecific.explanation: The first row has one cell with colspan="3" so it stretches across all 3 columns. The rest of the table is a normal 3-column structure.

topicSpecific.question: Where should the caption tag be placed in a table?
topicSpecific.hint: It is the title of the table.
topicSpecific.answer: Right after the opening <table> tag, before any <tr>.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <caption> must be the first child of <table>. Browsers display it above the table as the title.

topicSpecific.question: Ananya is using a table to place a logo and a menu side by side. Explain why this is wrong and what she should use.
topicSpecific.hint: Tables are for data only.
topicSpecific.answer: Tables should not be used for page layout. Ananya should use semantic tags like <header> with a logo div and a <nav>, and arrange them using CSS Flexbox or Grid.
topicSpecific.explanation: Table layouts are bad for accessibility (screen readers get confused), bad for mobile (hard to make responsive), and bad for SEO. Modern websites use CSS layout systems.

topicSpecific.question: Create a description list for FAQs. Question 1: 'What is HTML?' Answer: 'A markup language for web pages.' Question 2: 'Is HTML hard?' Answer: 'No, it is beginner-friendly.'
topicSpecific.hint: Use dt for questions and dd for answers.
topicSpecific.answer:
<dl>
  <dt>What is HTML?</dt>
  <dd>A markup language for web pages.</dd>
  <dt>Is HTML hard?</dt>
  <dd>No, it is beginner-friendly.</dd>
</dl>
topicSpecific.explanation: Description lists work well for FAQs. Each question is a <dt>, each answer is a <dd>.

topicSpecific.hint: The 3rd column is filled by rowspan. How many columns are left?
topicSpecific.answer: 3 td cells (columns 1, 2, and 4 need cells; column 3 is already taken by rowspan).
topicSpecific.explanation: Row 1 has colspan=2 (cols 1-2), rowspan=2 (col 3), and one normal cell (col 4). In row 2, col 3 is already filled, so you need cells for cols 1, 2, and 4 — that is 3 td cells.

topicSpecific.question: Build a complete HTML page with a table showing 4 students and their marks in 3 subjects (Maths, Science, English). Include caption 'Term 1 Results', thead, tbody, and a tfoot row showing the class average for each subject.
topicSpecific.hint: Use caption, thead, tbody, tfoot, and calculate averages manually.
topicSpecific.explanation: Full table with all major parts. Caption is the title, thead holds headers, tbody holds 4 student rows, tfoot holds the averages calculated from the data.

topicSpecific.hint: The ol is in the wrong place.
topicSpecific.answer: The nested <ol> is a direct child of <ul>, which is invalid. It must be inside an <li>.
topicSpecific.explanation: Only <li> elements can be direct children of <ul> or <ol>. Nested lists must be placed inside a list item. The correct structure puts the inner list inside 'Item 1'.

mixed.question: Which tag would you use for a list of 5 hobbies in no particular order?
mixed.hint: The key phrase is 'no particular order'.
mixed.explanation: Unordered lists (<ul>) are used when the order does not matter. If there were a ranking, you would use <ol>.

mixed.question: Write HTML for a numbered list of 3 goals for the year.
mixed.answer:
<ol>
  <li>Learn HTML and CSS</li>
  <li>Build my first website</li>
  <li>Publish it online</li>
</ol>
mixed.explanation: Ordered list because goals are often ranked or done in a sequence.

mixed.question: What tag is used for the title of a table?
mixed.hint: It goes right after the opening table tag.
mixed.explanation: The caption tag holds the title of a table. Browsers display it above (or sometimes below) the table.

mixed.question: Create a simple table with 2 columns (Name, Age) and 2 rows of data (Aarav 13, Priya 14). Include a header row.
mixed.hint: One tr for headers with th, then two tr rows with td.
mixed.explanation: 3 total rows: the first uses th for headers, and the next two use td for data.

mixed.question: What is the difference between <ul> and <ol>?
mixed.hint: Think about the markers they use.
mixed.explanation: Use <ul> when order does not matter and <ol> when it does. Both use <li> for items.

mixed.question:
Why is this list wrong?
<ol>
  Apple
  Banana
  Cherry
</ol>
mixed.hint: Every item needs its own tag.
mixed.answer: Each item must be wrapped in <li>. Without li, the items don't form a list — they just appear as plain text.
mixed.explanation: Lists require <li> tags for each item. Plain text inside <ol> or <ul> is invalid.

mixed.question: Build a table with 4 columns and 3 rows. The first row is a header that spans all 4 columns with the word 'Menu'. Below that, columns: Item, Price, Category, Available.
mixed.explanation: The first row has one th with colspan=4 to stretch across all columns. The rest follows the usual table structure.
type="i" uses lowercase Roman numerals. start="2" begins at 2, which in Roman is ii. Next is iii.

mixed.question: Create a 3-level nested list. Level 1: Continents (Asia). Level 2: Countries (India). Level 3: Cities (Delhi, Mumbai).
mixed.hint: Nest a ul inside an li, and another ul inside that.
mixed.explanation: Each level of nesting goes inside the parent li. The deeper you go, the more indented the list appears.

mixed.question: In a table row, what is the main visual difference between <th> and <td>?
mixed.answer: <th> content is bold and centered by default. <td> content is normal weight and left-aligned.
mixed.explanation: Browsers style table headers (th) as bold and centered so they stand out from data cells (td). This helps users scan the table faster.

mixed.answer: The row uses colspan=2, making B take 2 columns. Total columns = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4, but the table only has 3. This shifts things or widens the table to 4 columns.
mixed.explanation: When using colspan, make sure the total width of the row equals the number of columns in the table. For a 3-column table, options are 1+1+1, 2+1, or 3.

mixed.question: Build a complete HTML page with a styled table (borders, padding, alternating row colors) showing 4 rows of sample data: name and score. Use CSS in the head.
mixed.hint: Use border-collapse, border, padding, and nth-child for stripes.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  table { border-collapse: collapse; margin: 20px; }
  th, td { border: 1px solid #333; padding: 10px; }
  tr:nth-child(even) { background-color: #f0f0f0; }
  th { background-color: #d0e0ff; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
<table>
  <tr><th>Name</th><th>Score</th></tr>
  <tr><td>Aarav</td><td>92</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Priya</td><td>95</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Rohan</td><td>78</td></tr>
  <tr><td>Ananya</td><td>85</td></tr>
</table>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: border-collapse makes cell borders merge into single lines. tr:nth-child(even) selects every other row for the zebra-stripe effect.

mixed.question: How many TD tags need to be written across all rows for a 3-column, 3-row table where the top-left cell has rowspan=2?
mixed.hint: Row 1 has 3 cells. Row 2 has 2 cells (one is filled). Row 3 has 3 cells.
mixed.explanation: Row 1: 3 cells including the rowspan cell. Row 2: only 2 cells because the first column is already filled. Row 3: 3 normal cells. Total = 8.

mixed.question: Create a nested unordered list for a 3-level course outline: 'HTML' (main), with sub-items 'Basics' and 'Advanced'. Under 'Basics', list 'Tags' and 'Attributes'. Under 'Advanced', list 'Forms' and 'Tables'.
mixed.explanation: Three levels: HTML > (Basics, Advanced) > (their sub-items). Each nested ul sits inside an li.

mixed.question: Which list type is best for displaying a glossary of web terms and their meanings?
mixed.explanation: Description lists with <dt> (term) and <dd> (definition) are specifically designed for glossaries, dictionaries, FAQs, and any content that pairs a term with its meaning.

mcqs.question: Which tag creates an unordered (bulleted) list?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. <ul> stands for unordered list and creates a bulleted list. <ol> is for numbered lists, <dl> is for description lists, and <list> is not a valid HTML tag.

mcqs.question: Which tag is used for each item inside a <ul> or <ol>?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. <li> (list item) is used for items in both ordered and unordered lists. <dt> is for terms in description lists, not for ul/ol.

mcqs.question: Which tag creates a table row?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <tr> stands for table row. <td> is a data cell, <th> is a header cell, and <row> is not a valid HTML tag.

mcqs.question: Which tag creates a table header cell (bold and centered by default)?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <th> creates a table header cell. Browsers automatically render its content in bold and center-aligned. <head> is the head of the HTML document (not a table cell).

mcqs.question: Which attribute makes a table cell span multiple columns?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. The colspan attribute makes a cell span multiple columns. For example, <td colspan="3"> makes the cell stretch across 3 columns.

mcqs.question: What does the tag <dl> stand for?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <dl> stands for description list. It is used with <dt> (description term) and <dd> (description description) for term-definition pairs.

mcqs.question: In an ordered list, what does type="A" do?

options.B: Uses capital letters (A, B, C) instead of numbers

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. type="A" changes the numbering style to capital letters A, B, C. It does not sort items alphabetically. Other values include a, I, i, and 1 (default).

mcqs.question: Which tag wraps the title of a table?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <caption> wraps the title of a table and must be the first child of <table>. <title> is for the browser tab title, not for tables.

mcqs.question:
What will this list show:
  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The reversed attribute counts backwards but does not reverse the items themselves. A is numbered 3, B is 2, and C is 1.

mcqs.question: Which tag groups the header rows of a table?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <thead> wraps the header rows of a table. It goes between <table> and <tbody>.

mcqs.question: For a list of ingredients in a recipe, which list is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Ingredients have no specific order, so an unordered list (<ul>) is correct. The steps of the recipe would use <ol>.

mcqs.question: Which tag is used for the definition (description) in a description list?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <dd> is for the description (definition). <dt> is the term being described. Both go inside <dl>.

mcqs.question:
What does this ordered list display?
  1. A
  2. B
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. start="10" makes the list begin at 10. The next item continues counting: 11.

mcqs.question: Which tag groups the body rows of a table?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <tbody> wraps the data rows of a table, separating them from the header (<thead>) and footer (<tfoot>).

mcqs.question: For summary rows (like totals at the bottom), which tag should you use?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. <tfoot> is specifically for footer rows in a table, commonly used for totals and summary data. <footer> is a semantic tag for page footers, not tables.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is INVALID inside a <ul>?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A <ul> can only contain <li> elements as direct children. Plain text or other tags directly inside ul are invalid.

mcqs.question: In a 3-column table, which row is correctly using rowspan?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. When a cell has rowspan=2, the next row's first column is already taken. So the next row needs only 2 td cells (for columns 2 and 3). Option A has 3 td in row 2, which would be wrong.

mcqs.question: What is wrong with using tables for page layout?

options.C: They hurt accessibility, mobile responsiveness, and SEO
options.D: Nothing, tables are fine for layout

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Table-based layouts are bad for screen readers (which read cell-by-cell), hard to make responsive for mobile, and confusing for search engines. Use CSS Flexbox or Grid for layout. Tables are still valid for actual data.

mcqs.question: Which list type would you use for a 'Top 10 Movies' ranking?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A ranking has an order (1st, 2nd, 3rd...), so <ol> is correct. You could also use reversed to count from 10 down to 1 for a countdown effect.

mcqs.question: What does <td colspan="2"> do?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. colspan="2" merges the cell across 2 columns, making it look like one wide cell. This is useful for headers that cover multiple columns.

mcqs.question: In a 4-column table, a row has A and B. How many columns are filled?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. colspan=2 fills 2 columns (1-2), and the second td fills 1 more (column 3). Total filled = 3 columns. Column 4 is left empty, so the row is incomplete and the table may render oddly.

mcqs.question: Which is the correct way to nest an ordered list inside an unordered list?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Nested lists always go inside a list item (<li>), never directly inside another <ul> or <ol>. A ul/ol may only have li as direct children.

mcqs.question:
What will
  1. First
  2. Second
display?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. type="i" is lowercase Roman numerals. Capital I would give I. First, II. Second.

mcqs.question: If a table has 3 rows and 3 columns, and the top-left cell uses rowspan="3", how many total <td> tags are needed?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Row 1: 3 td (including the rowspan cell). Row 2: 2 td (first column is filled). Row 3: 2 td. Total = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7.

mcqs.question: Which statement about <dl> is FALSE?

options.A: It is used for term-definition pairs

mcqs.explanation: D is correct (this statement is false). A single <dt> can have multiple <dd> tags if it has multiple definitions or descriptions. All the other statements are true.

codingChallenges.title: Favorite Books List
codingChallenges.description: Create a complete HTML page with a heading 'My Favorite Books' and an unordered list of at least 5 books you like.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A page with the heading and a bulleted list of 5 books.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use <ul> and <li>. Full HTML boilerplate (DOCTYPE, html, head, body).
codingChallenges.solution:
My Favorite Books

  • Wings of Fire - A. P. J. Abdul Kalam
  • The Alchemist - Paulo Coelho
  • Harry Potter - J. K. Rowling
  • Panchatantra - Vishnu Sharma
  • Malgudi Days - R. K. Narayan

codingChallenges.title: Recipe with Ordered Steps
codingChallenges.description: Create an HTML page for Priya's favourite recipe. Include the recipe name as an h1, an unordered list of ingredients, and an ordered list of steps.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A page with the recipe name, a bulleted ingredient list, and a numbered step list.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use both <ul> and <ol>. Include at least 4 ingredients and 4 steps.
codingChallenges.solution:
Priya's Masala Chai

Ingredients

  • 1 cup water
  • 1/2 cup milk
  • 1 teaspoon tea leaves
  • Sugar to taste
  • Crushed ginger and cardamom

Steps

  1. Boil water in a pan.
  2. Add ginger, cardamom, and tea leaves.
  3. Pour in milk and boil for 2 minutes.
  4. Strain into a cup and add sugar.

codingChallenges.title: Class Marks Table
codingChallenges.description: Build a table showing 4 students (Aarav, Priya, Rohan, Ananya) and their marks in 3 subjects (Maths, Science, English). Include a caption, a header row with th cells, and all data in a tbody.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A table with a title, headers, and 4 rows of student marks.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use caption, thead, tbody, and border="1". At least 4 students.

codingChallenges.title: Nested Menu for a Restaurant
codingChallenges.description: Create a nested list for a restaurant menu with 3 categories: Starters, Main Course, and Desserts. Each category should have at least 3 items nested inside.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: An outer list of 3 categories, each with an indented sub-list of items.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use nested <ul> tags. The nested ul must be inside the parent li.
codingChallenges.solution:
Spice Palace Menu

  • Starters
    • Samosa
    • Paneer Tikka
    • Vegetable Pakora
  • Main Course
    • Butter Chicken
    • Dal Makhani
    • Paneer Butter Masala
  • Desserts
    • Gulab Jamun
    • Rasmalai
    • Kulfi

codingChallenges.title: Timetable with colspan
codingChallenges.description: Create a school timetable with columns for Time and 3 days (Mon, Tue, Wed). Include a Lunch Break row that uses colspan=3 to span across all three days.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A timetable with a Lunch Break row that stretches across all 3 day columns.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use colspan. At least 4 rows including the lunch row.

codingChallenges.title: Glossary Description List
codingChallenges.description: Create a description list for at least 5 web development terms (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Browser, URL) with their meanings.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A glossary with 5 terms and their definitions.
codingChallenges.solution:
Web Development Glossary

HTML
HyperText Markup Language - the language used to structure web pages.
CSS
Cascading Style Sheets - used to style and lay out web pages.
JavaScript
A programming language that adds interactivity to web pages.
Browser
A program like Chrome or Firefox that displays web pages.
URL
Uniform Resource Locator - the address of a web page on the internet.

codingChallenges.title: Styled Comparison Table
codingChallenges.description: Build a beautiful styled table comparing 3 phones. Columns: Phone, Price, Battery, Camera, Rating. Use CSS in the head for borders, padding, header background, and alternating row colors.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A well-designed table with 3 phone rows, alternating row colors, and a colored header.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use internal CSS. Include border-collapse, padding, and nth-child striping.
codingChallenges.solution:
Best Phones 2026
PhonePriceBatteryCameraRating
Pixel 10600004500 mAh50 MP4.6
Galaxy S26750005000 mAh108 MP4.7
OnePlus 13550005200 mAh64 MP4.5

codingChallenges.title: Complex Timetable with rowspan and colspan
codingChallenges.description: Build a weekly class timetable that uses BOTH rowspan and colspan. Include at least one double-period class (rowspan=2) and at least one assembly row that spans all days (colspan).
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A timetable with merged cells for double periods and a full-width assembly row.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use both rowspan and colspan. At least 4 time slots.
codingChallenges.solution:
Rohan's Weekly Schedule
TimeMondayTuesdayWednesday
9:00 Morning Assembly (all days)
10:00 Maths Double Art Period English
11:00 Science History
12:00 Hindi Sports Music
